perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall only use Confidential Information for the purpose of negotiation and/or evaluation of any co-operation between the Parties. The Parties shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party, other than to their officers, employees or consultants (engaged in the matter of this Agreement) on a need to know basis, without the prior written approval of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may use Confidential Information for the general purpose of potential negotiation or evaluation of any possible co-operation between the Parties. The Parties will endeavor to avoid disclosing Confidential Information to any third party, other than to their officers, employees or consultants (engaged in the matter of this Agreement) on a need to know basis, without the prior written approval of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text strictly limits the use of Confidential Information to 'negotiation and/or evaluation'. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'may use' and 'general purpose of potential negotiation or evaluation' significantly broadening the permitted use. Replacing 'shall not disclose' with 'will endeavor to avoid disclosing' weakens the obligation. These changes allows for interpretation which is not in compliance with trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act (Sweden) requires reasonable steps to protect trade secrets. Loosening the restrictions on use and disclosure of confidential information undermines these required reasonable steps to protect the trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: bestämmelser Artikel 16 Sanktioner om detta direktiv inte efterlevs Medlemsstaterna ska se till att de behöriga rättsliga myndigheterna får ålägga alla personer som underlåter eller vägrar att efterleva åtgärder som antas enligt artiklarna 9, 10 och 12 sanktioner. Sanktionerna ska inbegripa möjligheten att förelägga viten i fall då en åtgärd som antagits i enlighet med artiklarna 10 och 12 inte efterlevs. Sanktionerna ska vara effektiva, proportionella och avskräckande. Artikel 17 Informationsutbyte och kontaktpersoner För att främja samarbete, inklusive informationsutbyte, dels mellan medlemsstaterna, dels mellan medlemsstaterna och kommissionen, ska varje medlemsstat utse en eller flera nationella kontaktpersoner för frågor som rör genomförandet av de åtgärder som anges i detta direktiv. De ska överlämna uppgifter om den eller de nationella kontaktpersonerna till övriga medlemsstater och till kommissionen. Artikel 18 Rapporter 1. Senast den 9 juni 2021 ska Europeiska unionens immaterialrättsmyndighet, inom ramen för verksamheten vid Europeiska observatoriet avseende intrång i immateriella rättigheter, utarbeta en första rapport om utvecklingstendenserna när det gäller rättstvister om olagligt anskaffande, utnyttjande eller röjande av företagshemligheter med tillämpning av detta direktiv. 2. Senast den 9 juni 2022 ska kommissionen utarbeta en lägesrapport om tillämpningen av detta direktiv och överlämna den till Europaparlamentet och rådet. I denna rapport ska vederbörlig hänsyn tas till den rapport som avses i punkt 1. I lägesrapporten ska särskilt behandlas de konsekvenser som tillämpningen av detta direktiv eventuellt har för forskning och innovation, arbetstagares rörlighet samt utövandet av rätten till yttrande- och informationsfrihet. 3. Senast den 9 juni 2026 ska kommissionen göra en utvärdering av konsekvenserna av detta direktiv och överlämna en rapport till Europaparlamentet och till rådet. Artikel 19 Införlivande 1. Medlemsstaterna ska senast den 9 juni 2018 sätta i kraft de bestämmelser i lagar och andra författningar som är nödvändiga för att följa detta direktiv. De ska genast överlämna texten till dessa bestämmelser till kommissionen. När en medlemsstat antar dessa bestämmelser ska de innehålla en hänvisning till detta direktiv eller åtföljas av en sådan hänvisning när de offentliggörs. Närmare föreskrifter om hur hänvisningen ska göras ska varje medlemsstat själv utfärda. 2. Medlemsstaterna ska till kommissionen överlämna texten till de centrala bestämmelser i nationell rätt som de antar inom det område som omfattas av detta direktiv. Artikel 20 Ikraftträdande Detta direktiv träder i kraft den tjugonde dagen efter det att det har offentliggjorts i Europeiska unionens officiella tidning . Artikel 21 Adressater Detta direktiv riktar sig till medlemsstaterna. Utfärdat i Strasbourg den 8 juni 2016. På Europaparlamentets vägnar M. SCHULZ Ordförande På rådets vägnar A.G. KOENDERS Ordförande ( 1 ) EUT C 226, 16.7.2014, s. 48 . ( 2 ) Europaparlamentets ståndpunkt av den 14 april 2016 (ännu ej offentliggjord i EUT) och rådets beslut av den 27 maj 2016. ( 3 ) Rådets beslut 94/800/EG av den 22 december 1994 om ingående, på Europeiska gemenskapens vägnar – vad beträffar frågor som omfattas av dess behörighet – av de avtal som är resultatet av de multilaterala förhandlingarna i Uruguayrundan (1986–1994) ( EGT L 336, 23.12.1994, s. 1 ). ( 4 ) Europaparlamentets och rådets förordning (EG) nr 1049/2001 av den 30 maj 2001 om allmänhetens tillgång till Europaparlamentets, rådets och kommissionens handlingar ( EGT L 145, 31.5.2001, s. 43 ). ( 5 ) Europaparlamentets och rådets förordning (EG) nr 1367/2006 av den 6 september 2006 om tillämpning av bestämmelserna i Århuskonventionen om tillgång till information, allmänhetens deltagande i beslutsprocesser och tillgång till rättslig prövning i miljöfrågor på gemenskapens institutioner och organ ( EUT L 264, 25.9.2006, s. 13 ). ( 6 ) Europaparlamentets och rådets direktiv 2003/4/EG av den
perturbation.justification: The original contract text imposes a strict obligation regarding the use and disclosure of confidential information, while the changed text weakens these obligations. The original text states 'shall only use' and 'shall not disclose', whereas the changed text uses 'may use' and 'will endeavor to avoid disclosing'. This creates ambiguity and weakens the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that the Trade Secrets Act (Sweden) requires reasonable steps to protect trade secrets and that loosening restrictions undermines these reasonable steps. Although there are no extracted law snippets, the shift from 'shall' to 'may' and 'endeavor to avoid' introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the clause, creating a significant contradiction with the intent to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall ensure that any of its officers, employees, and consultants that receive Confidential Information is bound by confidentiality undertakings that provide at least the same level of protection as is provided by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party should attempt to ensure that its officers, employees, and consultants that receive Confidential Information are generally aware of confidentiality considerations similar to those in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall ensure' to 'should attempt to ensure' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'bound by confidentiality undertakings that provide at least the same level of protection' with 'generally aware of confidentiality considerations similar to those in this Agreement' removes the requirement for legally binding agreements and creates a vague awareness standard. This contradicts the requirement to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act (Sweden) requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. This change undermines these measures by making the confidentiality undertakings optional and vaguely defined, which is not sufficient for trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall ensure' to 'should attempt to ensure' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Replacing legally binding agreements with a vague awareness standard undermines the measures needed for trade secret protection, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The altered text diminishes the original contract's intention to safeguard confidential information, potentially violating legal protection standards despite the absence of supporting legal snippets.

perturbation.original_text: Upon request of the Disclosing Party or upon the termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall return, delete or destroy (as instructed in writing by the Disclosing Party) all Confidential Information, and all copies thereof. The Receiving Party shall certify in writing that the information has been returned, deleted or destroyed (as applicable) and that no copies have been retained of any such Confidential Information, either partly or wholly.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party should endeavor to return, delete, or destroy (as reasonably interpreted by the Receiving Party) all Confidential Information, and all copies thereof. The Receiving Party may provide a statement that the information has been addressed (as applicable).
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the return, deletion, or destruction of confidential information. The modified text weakens this by changing 'shall return, delete or destroy' to 'should endeavor to return, delete, or destroy' and adding 'as reasonably interpreted by the Receiving Party', making the action discretionary. It also replaces the requirement for written certification with a discretionary statement. This fails to adequately safeguard trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act (Sweden) requires the implementation of reasonable protection measures. Changing the 'shall' obligation to a 'should endeavor' weakens the protection of trade secrets, as the destruction or return of confidential data is not guaranteed.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation to protect confidential information. The original text uses 'shall,' which is a mandatory term, while the changed text uses 'should endeavor,' making the action discretionary. Additionally, the requirement for written certification is replaced with a discretionary statement. The LLM's law explanation states that the Trade Secrets Act (Sweden) requires the implementation of reasonable protection measures. The changed text weakens these protections. Snippet 2 discusses sanctions for non-compliance, suggesting that the measures should be effective, proportional and dissuasive. The changes undermine the legal requirement for effective protection of trade secrets, as the destruction or return of confidential data is not guaranteed under the modified contract.

perturbation.original_text: In the case of any breach of any material obligations under this Agreement by the Receiving Party or any permitted third party who has received Confidential Information according to this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall be liable to pay liquidated damages to the Disclosing Party amounting to SEK [amount] for each such breach of its obligations.
perturbation.changed_text: In the case of any potential breach of any vaguely defined obligations under this Agreement by the Receiving Party or any permitted third party who has received Confidential Information according to this Agreement, the Receiving Party may be requested to consider paying some form of compensation to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates that a breach of material obligations leads to liquidated damages. The modified text changes 'any breach' to 'any potential breach', 'material obligations' to 'vaguely defined obligations,' 'shall be liable to pay liquidated damages' to 'may be requested to consider paying some form of compensation', which makes the obligation to pay damages discretionary and unclear in amount. This removes the disincentive against breaching the agreement, undermining the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act (Sweden) requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The modification removes the clear liability for breach and replaces it with a vague obligation which fails to adequately protect trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the clear obligation to pay liquidated damages for a breach of material obligations with a discretionary consideration of compensation for vaguely defined obligations. This undermines the protection of trade secrets, as it removes the disincentive against breaching the agreement. The original text stipulated a specific penalty (liquidated damages), while the modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion, potentially weakening the enforcement of trade secret protection. The provided snippet states that sanctions should be 'effective, proportionate and dissuasive', and the weakening of penalty does not provide a dissuasive measure, creating a legal contradiction.
